infobox.value: Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:IPA#Rendering_issues "Help:IPA"), you may see [question marks, boxes, or other symbols](/wiki/Specials_(Unicode_block)#Replacement_character "Specials (Unicode block)") instead of [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") characters. For an introductory guide on IPA symbols, see [Help:IPA](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA"). | |

(*italiano*, pronounced [[itaˈljaːno]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:It-italiano.ogg "File:It-italiano.ogg"), or *lingua italiana*, pronounced [[ˈliŋɡwa itaˈljaːna]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian")) is a [Romance language](/wiki/Romance_language "Romance language") of the [Indo-European language family](/wiki/Indo-European_language_family "Indo-European language family"). It evolved from the [Vulgar Latin](/wiki/Vulgar_Latin "Vulgar Latin") of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") and,[[7]](#cite_note-8) together with [Sardinian](/wiki/Sardinian_language "Sardinian language"), is the least differentiated language from Latin.[[8]](#cite_note-9) Current estimates indicate that between 68 and 85 million people speak Italian, including approximately 64 million native speakers as of 2024.

Romance language

This article is about the Italian language. For the regional varieties of standard Italian, see [Regional Italian](/wiki/Regional_Italian "Regional Italian").

**Italian** (*italiano*, pronounced [[itaˈljaːno]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:It-italiano.ogg "File:It-italiano.ogg"), or *lingua italiana*, pronounced [[ˈliŋɡwa itaˈljaːna]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian")) is a [Romance language](/wiki/Romance_language "Romance language") of the [Indo-European language family](/wiki/Indo-European_language_family "Indo-European language family"). It evolved from the [Vulgar Latin](/wiki/Vulgar_Latin "Vulgar Latin") of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") and,[[7]](#cite_note-8) together with [Sardinian](/wiki/Sardinian_language "Sardinian language"), is the least differentiated language from Latin.[[8]](#cite_note-9) Current estimates indicate that between 68 and 85 million people speak Italian, including approximately 64 million native speakers as of 2024.

Italian is an [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [San Marino](/wiki/San_Marino "San Marino"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") ([Ticino](/wiki/Ticino "Ticino") and part of the [Grisons](/wiki/Italian_Grisons "Italian Grisons")), and [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City"), and it has official [minority status](/wiki/Minority_language "Minority language") in [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia"), [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") ([Istria](/wiki/Slovene_Istria "Slovene Istria")), [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania"),[[6]](#cite_note-coe-6)[[9]](#cite_note-10) [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina"),[[6]](#cite_note-coe-6) and in [6 municipalities of Brazil](/wiki/Languages_of_Brazil#Language_co-officialization "Languages of Brazil").[[10]](#cite_note-encantado-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12) It is also spoken in other European and non-EU countries, most notably in [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta") (by 66% of the population),[[12]](#cite_note-13) [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania") (upwards of 70%),[[13]](#cite_note-Zonova,_Tatiana_2013-14) and [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco"),[[2]](#cite_note-Italian_language-2) as well as by large [immigrant and expatriate communities](/wiki/Italian_diaspora "Italian diaspora") in the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), and on other continents.[[1]](#cite_note-e25-1)

Italian is a major [language in Europe](/wiki/European_language "European language"), being one of the official languages of the [Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe") and one of the working languages of the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe"). It is the third-most-widely spoken native [language in the European Union](/wiki/Languages_of_the_European_Union "Languages of the European Union") (13% of the EU population) and it is spoken as a second language by 13 million EU citizens (3%).[[14]](#cite_note-europa2024-15)[[15]](#cite_note-forbes-16)[[16]](#cite_note-europa2012-17) Italian is the main working language of the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See"), serving as the [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") in the [Roman Catholic hierarchy](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_hierarchy "Roman Catholic hierarchy") and the official language of the [Sovereign Military Order of Malta](/wiki/Sovereign_Military_Order_of_Malta "Sovereign Military Order of Malta"). Italian influence led to the development of [derivated languages and dialects](#Influence_and_derived_languages) worldwide. It is also widespread in various sectors and markets, with [its loanwords](/wiki/List_of_English_words_of_Italian_origin "List of English words of Italian origin") used in arts, luxury goods, fashion, sports and cuisine; it has a significant use in [musical terminology](/wiki/Musical_terminology "Musical terminology") and [opera](/wiki/Opera "Opera"), with numerous Italian words referring to music that have become international terms taken into various languages worldwide, including [in English](/wiki/List_of_Italian_musical_terms_used_in_English "List of Italian musical terms used in English").[[17]](#cite_note-18)

Italian is considered a conservative Romance language in phonology, lexicon, and morphology. Almost all native Italian words end with [vowels](/wiki/Vowel "Vowel"), and the language has a 7-vowel [sound system](/wiki/Sound_system_(linguistics) "Sound system (linguistics)") ("e" and "o" have mid-low and mid-high sounds).[[18]](#cite_note-19) Italian has contrast between short and [long consonants](/wiki/Consonant_length "Consonant length") and [gemination](/wiki/Gemination "Gemination") (doubling) of consonants.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Italian_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

"History of Italian" redirects here. For the history of the Italian people, see [Italians](/wiki/Italians "Italians"). For the history of the Italian culture, see [culture of Italy](/wiki/Culture_of_Italy "Culture of Italy").

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Italian_language&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origins")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Indovinello_veronese.jpg/250px-Indovinello_veronese.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indovinello_veronese.jpg)

The [Veronese Riddle](/wiki/Veronese_Riddle "Veronese Riddle") (c. 8th or early 9th century), a riddle reflecting either a form of Medieval Latin or the earliest extant example of Romance vernacular in Italy

The Italian language has developed through a long and slow process, which began after the [Western Roman Empire's fall](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire") and the onset of the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") in the 5th century.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

Latin, the predominant language of the western Roman Empire, remained the established written language in Europe during the Middle Ages, although most people were illiterate. Over centuries, the [Vulgar Latin](/wiki/Vulgar_Latin "Vulgar Latin") popularly spoken in various areas of Europe—including the [Italian peninsula](/wiki/Italian_peninsula "Italian peninsula")—evolved into local varieties, or dialects, unaffected by formal standards and teachings. These varieties are not in any sense "dialects" of standard Italian, which itself started off as one of these local tongues, but [sister languages](/wiki/Sister_language "Sister language") of Italian.[[20]](#cite_note-:0-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22) The Latin-speaking class referred to the collective Romance vernaculars of Europe as *Romanz*, *Romance*, or, in Italy, *Romanzo* or *Volgare*.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

The linguistic and historical demarcations between late Vulgar Latin and early Romance varieties in Italy are imprecise. The earliest surviving texts that can definitely be called vernacular (as distinct from its predecessor Vulgar Latin) are legal formulae known as the [Placiti Cassinesi](/wiki/Placiti_Cassinesi "Placiti Cassinesi") from the [province of Benevento](/wiki/Duchy_of_Benevento "Duchy of Benevento") that date from 960 to 963, although the [Veronese Riddle](/wiki/Veronese_Riddle "Veronese Riddle"), probably from the 8th or early 9th century, contains a late form of Vulgar Latin that can be seen as a very early sample of a vernacular dialect of Italy.[[23]](#cite_note-24) The [Commodilla catacomb inscription](/wiki/Commodilla_catacomb_inscription "Commodilla catacomb inscription") likewise probably dates to the early 9th century and appears to reflect a language somewhere between late Vulgar Latin and early vernacular.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Dante03.jpg/250px-Dante03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dante03.jpg)

[Dante Alighieri](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri"), whose works helped establish modern Italian language, is considered one of the greatest poets of the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"). His epic poem *[Divine Comedy](/wiki/Divine_Comedy "Divine Comedy")* ranks among the finest works of [world literature](/wiki/World_literature "World literature").[[24]](#cite_note-25)

The language that came to be thought of as Italian developed in central Tuscany and was first formalized in the early 14th century through the works of Tuscan writer [Dante Alighieri](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri"), written in his native [Florentine](/wiki/Florentine_dialect "Florentine dialect"). Dante's [epic poems](/wiki/Epic_poems "Epic poems"), known collectively as the *[Commedia](/wiki/Divine_Comedy "Divine Comedy")*, to which another Tuscan poet [Giovanni Boccaccio](/wiki/Giovanni_Boccaccio "Giovanni Boccaccio") later affixed the title *Divina*, were read throughout the Italian peninsula. His written vernacular became the touchstone for elaborating a "canonical standard" that all educated Italians could understand. The poetry of [Petrarch](/wiki/Petrarch "Petrarch") was also widely admired and influential in the development of the literary language, and would be identified as a model for vernacular writing by [Pietro Bembo](/wiki/Pietro_Bembo "Pietro Bembo") in the 16th century.

In addition to the widespread exposure gained through literature, Florentine also gained prestige due to the political and cultural significance of Florence at the time and the fact that it was linguistically a middle way between the northern and the southern Italian dialects.

Italian was progressively made an official language of most of the Italian states predating unification, slowly replacing Latin, even when ruled by foreign powers (such as Spain in the [Kingdom of Naples](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Naples "Kingdom of Naples"), or Austria in the [Kingdom of Lombardy–Venetia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Lombardy%E2%80%93Venetia "Kingdom of Lombardy–Venetia")), although the masses kept speaking primarily their local vernaculars. Italian was also one of the many recognised languages in the [Austro-Hungarian Empire](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Empire "Austro-Hungarian Empire").

Italy has always had a distinctive vernacular for each city because the cities, until recently, were thought of as [city-states](/wiki/City-state#Italy "City-state"). Those local languages have considerable [variety](/wiki/Variety_(linguistics) "Variety (linguistics)"), and as Tuscan-derived Italian came to be used throughout Italy, features of local speech were naturally adopted, producing various versions of [Regional Italian](/wiki/Regional_Italian "Regional Italian"). The most characteristic differences, for instance, between [Roman](/wiki/Rome "Rome") Italian and [Milanese](/wiki/Milan "Milan") Italian are [syntactic gemination](/wiki/Syntactic_gemination "Syntactic gemination") of initial [consonants](/wiki/Consonant "Consonant") in some contexts and the pronunciation of stressed "e", and of "s" between vowels in many words: e.g. *va bene* 'all right' is pronounced [vabˈbɛːne] by a Roman (and by any standard Italian speaker), [vaˈbeːne] by a Milanese (and by any speaker whose native dialect lies to the north of the [La Spezia–Rimini Line](/wiki/La_Spezia%E2%80%93Rimini_Line "La Spezia–Rimini Line")); *a casa* 'at home' is [akˈkaːsa] for Roman, [akˈkaːsa] or [akˈkaːza] for standard, [aˈkaːza] for Milanese and generally northern.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBerloco2018-26)

In contrast to the [Gallo-Italic linguistic panorama](/wiki/Gallo-Italic_languages "Gallo-Italic languages") of northern Italy, the [Italo-Dalmatian](/wiki/Italo-Dalmatian "Italo-Dalmatian"), [Neapolitan](/wiki/Neapolitan_language "Neapolitan language") and its related dialects were largely unaffected by the Franco-[Occitan](/wiki/Occitan_language "Occitan language") influences introduced to Italy mainly by [bards](/wiki/Bard "Bard") from France during the Middle Ages, but after the [Norman conquest of southern Italy](/wiki/Norman_conquest_of_southern_Italy "Norman conquest of southern Italy"), Sicily became the first Italian land to adopt Occitan lyric moods (and words) in poetry. Even in the case of northern Italian languages, however, scholars are careful not to overstate the effects of outsiders on the natural indigenous developments of the languages.

The economic might and relatively advanced development of Tuscany at the time ([Late Middle Ages](/wiki/Late_Middle_Ages "Late Middle Ages")) gave its language weight, although [Venetian](/wiki/Venetian_language "Venetian language") remained widespread in medieval Italian commercial life, and [Ligurian (or Genoese)](/wiki/Ligurian_language_(Romance) "Ligurian language (Romance)") remained in use in maritime trade alongside the Mediterranean. The increasing political and cultural relevance of Florence during the periods of the rise of the [Medici Bank](/wiki/Medici_Bank "Medici Bank"), [humanism](/wiki/Renaissance_humanism "Renaissance humanism"), and the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") made its dialect, or rather a refined version of it, a standard in the arts.

### Renaissance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Italian_language&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Renaissance")]

The [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") era, known as *il Rinascimento* in Italian, was seen as a time of rebirth, which is the literal meaning of both *renaissance* (from French) and *rinascimento* (Italian). Among its many manifestations, the Renaissance saw a reinvigorated interest in both classical antiquity and vernacular literature.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

Advancements in technology played a crucial role in the diffusion of the Italian language. The [printing press](/wiki/Printing_press "Printing press") was invented in the 15th century, and spread rapidly. By the year 1500, there were 56 printing presses in Italy, more than anywhere else in Europe. The printing press enabled the production of literature and documents in higher volumes and at lower cost, further accelerating the spread of Italian.[[27]](#cite_note-28)

Italian became the language used in the courts of every state in the [Italian peninsula](/wiki/Italian_peninsula "Italian peninsula"), and the [prestige variety](/wiki/Prestige_variety "Prestige variety") used on the island of [Corsica](/wiki/Corsica "Corsica")[[28]](#cite_note-29) (but not in the neighbouring [Sardinia](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia"), which on the contrary underwent [Italianization](/wiki/Italianization "Italianization") well into the late 18th century, under [Savoyard](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy") sway: the island's linguistic composition, roofed by the prestige of Spanish among the [Sardinians](/wiki/Sardinians "Sardinians"), would therein make for a rather slow process of [assimilation](/wiki/Cultural_assimilation "Cultural assimilation") to the Italian cultural sphere[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31)). The rediscovery of Dante's *[De vulgari eloquentia](/wiki/De_vulgari_eloquentia "De vulgari eloquentia")*, and a renewed interest in linguistics in the 16th century, sparked a debate that raged throughout Italy concerning the criteria that should govern the establishment of a modern Italian literary and spoken language. This discussion, known as [questione della lingua](/wiki/Questione_della_lingua "Questione della lingua") (i.e., the *problem of the language*), ran through the Italian culture until the end of the 19th century, often linked to the political debate on achieving a united Italian state. Renaissance scholars divided into three main factions:

* The [purists](/wiki/Purism_(language) "Purism (language)"), headed by Venetian [Pietro Bembo](/wiki/Pietro_Bembo "Pietro Bembo") (who, in his *[Gli Asolani](/wiki/Gli_Asolani "Gli Asolani")*, claimed the language might be based only on the great literary classics, such as [Petrarch](/wiki/Petrarch "Petrarch") and some part of Boccaccio). The purists thought the *[Divine Comedy](/wiki/Divine_Comedy "Divine Comedy")* was not dignified enough because it used elements from non-lyric registers of the language.
* [Niccolò Machiavelli](/wiki/Niccol%C3%B2_Machiavelli "Niccolò Machiavelli") and other [Florentines](/wiki/Florence "Florence") preferred the version spoken by ordinary people in their own times.
* The [courtiers](/wiki/Courtier "Courtier"), such as [Baldassare Castiglione](/wiki/Baldassare_Castiglione "Baldassare Castiglione") and [Gian Giorgio Trissino](/wiki/Gian_Giorgio_Trissino "Gian Giorgio Trissino"), insisted that each local vernacular contribute to the new standard.

A fourth faction claimed that the best Italian was the one that the papal court adopted, which was a mixture of the [Tuscan](/wiki/Tuscan_dialect "Tuscan dialect") and [Roman](/wiki/Roman_dialect "Roman dialect") dialects.[[31]](#cite_note-32) Eventually, Bembo's ideas prevailed, and the foundation of the [Accademia della Crusca](/wiki/Accademia_della_Crusca "Accademia della Crusca") in Florence (1582–1583), the official legislative body of the Italian language, led to the publication of [Agnolo Monosini](/wiki/Agnolo_Monosini "Agnolo Monosini")'s Latin tome *[Floris italicae linguae libri novem](/wiki/Floris_Italicae_lingue_libri_novem "Floris Italicae lingue libri novem")* in 1604 followed by the first Italian dictionary in 1612.

### Modern era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Italian_language&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Modern era")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Italian_language "Special:EditPage/Italian language") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(January 2026)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

An important event that helped the diffusion of Italian was the conquest and occupation of Italy by [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") (himself of Italian-Corsican descent) in the early 19th century. This conquest propelled the [unification of Italy](/wiki/Unification_of_Italy "Unification of Italy") some decades after and pushed the Italian language into the status of a [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca"), used not only among clerks, nobility, and functionaries in the Italian courts, but also by the [bourgeoisie](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie").

Today Italy has reached linguistic unity and an overwhelming majority of its 56 million citizens speak Italian. Many dialects are still alive, especially by the older generations. Today, Italian is one of the most studied foreign languages in the world.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

### Contemporary times

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Italian_language&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Contemporary times")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Francesco_Hayez_-_Ritratto_di_Alessandro_Manzoni.jpg/250px-Francesco_Hayez_-_Ritratto_di_Alessandro_Manzoni.jpg)](/wiki/File:Francesco_Hayez_-_Ritratto_di_Alessandro_Manzoni.jpg)

[Alessandro Manzoni](/wiki/Alessandro_Manzoni "Alessandro Manzoni") is famous for the novel *[The Betrothed](/wiki/The_Betrothed "The Betrothed")* (1827), ranked among the masterpieces of world literature.[[33]](#cite_note-britannica-34) He contributed to the nationwide use of the Italian language.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

The publication of Italian literature's first modern novel, *I promessi sposi* (*[The Betrothed](/wiki/The_Betrothed "The Betrothed")*) by [Alessandro Manzoni](/wiki/Alessandro_Manzoni "Alessandro Manzoni"), both reflected and furthered the growing trend towards Italian as a national standard language. Manzoni, a Milanesian, chose to write the book in the Florentine dialect, describing this choice, in the preface to his 1840 edition, as "rinsing" his Milanese "in the waters of the [Arno](/wiki/Arno "Arno")" ([Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence")'s river). The novel is commonly described as "the most widely read work in the Italian language".[[35]](#cite_note-Betrothed-36) It became a model for subsequent Italian literary fiction,[[35]](#cite_note-Betrothed-36) helping to galvanize national linguistic unity around the Florentine dialect.

This growth was initially relative; linguistic diversity continued during the unification of Italy (1848–1871). The Italian linguist [Tullio De Mauro](/wiki/Tullio_De_Mauro "Tullio De Mauro") estimated that only 2.5% of Italy's population could speak the Italian standardized language properly in 1861,[[36]](#cite_note-37) while Arrigo Castellani estimated the same value as 10%.[[37]](#cite_note-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39)

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Italian_language&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Classification")]

Italian is a [Romance language](/wiki/Romance_language "Romance language"), a descendant of [Vulgar Latin](/wiki/Vulgar_Latin "Vulgar Latin") (colloquial spoken Latin). Standard Italian is based on [Tuscan](/wiki/Tuscan_language "Tuscan language"), especially its [Florentine dialect](/wiki/Florentine_dialect "Florentine dialect"), and is, therefore, an [Italo-Dalmatian language](/wiki/Italo-Dalmatian_language "Italo-Dalmatian language"), a classification that includes most other central and southern Italian languages and the extinct [Dalmatian](/wiki/Dalmatian_language "Dalmatian language"). As in most Romance languages, [stress](/wiki/Stress_(linguistics) "Stress (linguistics)") is distinctive in Italian.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

According to *[Ethnologue](/wiki/Ethnologue "Ethnologue")*, [lexical similarity](/wiki/Lexical_similarity "Lexical similarity") is 89% with French, 87% with [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language"), 85% with [Sardinian](/wiki/Sardinian_language "Sardinian language"), 82% with Spanish, 82% with [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), 78% with [Ladin](/wiki/Ladin_language "Ladin language"), 77% with [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language").[[1]](#cite_note-e25-1) Estimates may differ according to sources.[[40]](#cite_note-ezglot.com-41)

A 1949 study by the linguist [Mario Pei](/wiki/Mario_Pei "Mario Pei") concluded that out of seven Romance languages, Italian's stressed vowel phonology was the second-closest to that of Classical Latin (after [Logudorese Sardinian](/wiki/Logudorese_Sardinian "Logudorese Sardinian")).[[41]](#cite_note-42) The study emphasized, however, that it represented only "a very elementary, incomplete and tentative demonstration" of how statistical methods could measure linguistic change, assigned "frankly arbitrary" point values to various types of change, and did not compare languages in the sample with respect to any characteristics or forms of divergence other than stressed vowels, among other caveats.[[42]](#cite_note-43)[[43]](#cite_note-44)

## Geographic distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Italian_language&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Geographic distribution")]

Main article: [Geographical distribution of Italian speakers](/wiki/Geographical_distribution_of_Italian_speakers "Geographical distribution of Italian speakers")

See also: [Italian language in Argentina](/wiki/Italian_language_in_Argentina "Italian language in Argentina"), [Italian language in Brazil](/wiki/Italian_language_in_Brazil "Italian language in Brazil"), [Italian language in Canada](/wiki/Italian_language_in_Canada "Italian language in Canada"), [Italian language in Croatia](/wiki/Italian_language_in_Croatia "Italian language in Croatia"), [Maltese Italian](/wiki/Maltese_Italian "Maltese Italian"), [Italian language in Slovenia](/wiki/Italian_language_in_Slovenia "Italian language in Slovenia"), [Swiss Italian](/wiki/Swiss_Italian "Swiss Italian"), [Italian language in the United States](/wiki/Italian_language_in_the_United_States "Italian language in the United States"), and [Italian language in Venezuela](/wiki/Italian_language_in_Venezuela "Italian language in Venezuela")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Suisse_italiene.png/250px-Suisse_italiene.png)](/wiki/File:Suisse_italiene.png)

[Italian language in Switzerland](/wiki/Swiss_Italian "Swiss Italian")

Italian is the official language of Italy and [San Marino](/wiki/San_Marino "San Marino") and is spoken fluently by the majority of the countries' populations. Italian is the third most spoken language in [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") (after German and French; see [Swiss Italian](/wiki/Swiss_Italian "Swiss Italian")). It is official both on the national level and on regional level in two [cantons](/wiki/Canton_of_Switzerland "Canton of Switzerland"): [Ticino](/wiki/Ticino "Ticino") and Grisons. Ticino, which includes [Lugano](/wiki/Lugano "Lugano"), the largest Italian-speaking city outside Italy, is the only canton where Italian is predominant.[[44]](#cite_note-45) Italian is also used in administration and official documents in [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City").[[45]](#cite_note-46)

Italian is also spoken by a minority in [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco") and France, especially in the southeastern part of the country.[[46]](#cite_note-2008census-47)[[1]](#cite_note-e25-1) Italian was the official language in [Savoy](/wiki/Savoy "Savoy") and in [Nice](/wiki/Nice "Nice") until 1860, when they were both annexed by France under the [Treaty of Turin](/wiki/Treaty_of_Turin_(1860) "Treaty of Turin (1860)"), a development that triggered the "[Niçard exodus](/wiki/Ni%C3%A7ard_exodus "Niçard exodus")", or the emigration of a quarter of the [Niçard Italians](/wiki/Ni%C3%A7ard_Italians "Niçard Italians") to Italy,[[47]](#cite_note-48) and the [Niçard Vespers](/wiki/Ni%C3%A7ard_Vespers "Niçard Vespers"). [Giuseppe Garibaldi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Garibaldi "Giuseppe Garibaldi") complained about the referendum that allowed France to annex Savoy and Nice, and a group of his followers (among the [Italian Savoyards](/wiki/Italian_Savoyards "Italian Savoyards")) took refuge in Italy in the following years. [Corsica](/wiki/Corsica "Corsica") passed from the [Republic of Genoa](/wiki/Republic_of_Genoa "Republic of Genoa") to France in 1769 after the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles_(1768) "Treaty of Versailles (1768)"). Italian was the official language of [Corsica](/wiki/Corsica "Corsica") until 1859.[[48]](#cite_note-49) Giuseppe Garibaldi called for the inclusion of the "[Corsican Italians](/wiki/Corsican_Italians "Corsican Italians")" within Italy when [Rome was annexed](/wiki/Capture_of_Rome "Capture of Rome") to the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy"), but King [Victor Emmanuel II](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_II "Victor Emmanuel II") did not agree to it. Italian is generally understood in Corsica by the population resident therein who speak [Corsican](/wiki/Corsican_language "Corsican language"), which is an Italo-Romance language similar to Tuscan.[[49]](#cite_note-Corsican-50) [Francization](/wiki/Francization "Francization") occurred in Nice case, and caused a near-disappearance of the Italian language as many of the Italian speakers in these areas migrated to Italy.[[50]](#cite_note-51)[[51]](#cite_note-rivistaetnie-52) In Corsica, on the other hand, almost everyone still speaks the [Corsican language](/wiki/Corsican_language "Corsican language"), which, due to its linguistic proximity to the Italian standard language, appears both linguistically as an Italian dialect and therefore as a carrier of Italian culture, despite the French government's decades-long efforts to cut Corsica off from the Italian motherland. Italian was the official language in [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco") until 1860, when it was replaced by the French.[[52]](#cite_note-miglioverde-53) This was due to the annexation of the surrounding [County of Nice](/wiki/County_of_Nice "County of Nice") to France following the [Treaty of Turin (1860)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Turin_(1860) "Treaty of Turin (1860)").[[52]](#cite_note-miglioverde-53)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Istria-Italians-1991.gif/250px-Istria-Italians-1991.gif)](/wiki/File:Istria-Italians-1991.gif)

Percent of inhabitants with Italian native tongue in [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia")'s and [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia")'s [Istria](/wiki/Istria "Istria")

It formerly had official status in [Montenegro](/wiki/Montenegro "Montenegro") (because of the [Venetian Albania](/wiki/Venetian_Albania "Venetian Albania")), parts of [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") and [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") (because of the [Venetian Istria](/wiki/Venetian_Istria "Venetian Istria") and [Venetian Dalmatia](/wiki/Venetian_Dalmatia "Venetian Dalmatia")), parts of [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") (because of the [Venetian rule in the Ionian Islands](/wiki/Venetian_rule_in_the_Ionian_Islands "Venetian rule in the Ionian Islands") and by the [Kingdom of Italy in the Dodecanese](/wiki/Italian_Islands_of_the_Aegean "Italian Islands of the Aegean")). Italian is widely spoken in [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta"), where nearly two-thirds of the population can speak it fluently (see [Maltese Italian](/wiki/Maltese_Italian "Maltese Italian")).[[53]](#cite_note-Europoll-54) Italian served as Malta's official language until 1934, when it was abolished by the British colonial administration amid strong local opposition.[[54]](#cite_note-55) [Italian language in Slovenia](/wiki/Italian_language_in_Slovenia "Italian language in Slovenia") is an officially recognised [minority language](/wiki/Minority_language "Minority language") in the country.[[55]](#cite_note-ita-slo-56) The official census, carried out in 2002, reported 2,258 ethnic Italians ([Istrian Italians](/wiki/Istrian_Italians "Istrian Italians")) in [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") (0.11% of the total population).[[56]](#cite_note-stat-57) [Italian language in Croatia](/wiki/Italian_language_in_Croatia "Italian language in Croatia") is an official minority language in the country, with many schools and public announcements published in both languages.[[55]](#cite_note-ita-slo-56) The 2001 census in [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") reported 19,636 ethnic Italians (Istrian Italians and [Dalmatian Italians](/wiki/Dalmatian_Italians "Dalmatian Italians")) in the country (some 0.42% of the total population).[[57]](#cite_note-dzs-58) Their numbers dropped dramatically after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") following the [Istrian–Dalmatian exodus](/wiki/Istrian%E2%80%93Dalmatian_exodus "Istrian–Dalmatian exodus"), which caused the emigration of between 230,000 and 350,000 Istrian Italians and Dalmatian Italians.[[58]](#cite_note-59)[[59]](#cite_note-query.nytimes.com-60) Italian was the official language of the [Republic of Ragusa](/wiki/Republic_of_Ragusa "Republic of Ragusa") from 1492 to 1807.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/39/Italian_empire_1940.PNG)](/wiki/File:Italian_empire_1940.PNG)

Italy and [its colonial possessions](/wiki/Italian_Empire "Italian Empire") in 1940

It formerly had official status in [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania") due to the [annexation of the country to the Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_Albania "Italian invasion of Albania") (1939–1943). Albania has a large population of non-native speakers, with over half of the population having some knowledge of the Italian language.[[13]](#cite_note-Zonova,_Tatiana_2013-14) The Albanian government has pushed to make Italian a compulsory second language in schools.[[61]](#cite_note-62) The Italian language is well-known and studied in Albania,[[62]](#cite_note-63) due to its historical ties and geographical proximity to Italy and to the diffusion of Italian television in the country.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

Due to heavy Italian influence during the [Italian colonial period](/wiki/Italian_Empire "Italian Empire"), Italian is still understood by some in former colonies such as Libya.[[1]](#cite_note-e25-1) Although it was the primary language in [Libya](/wiki/Libya "Libya") since [colonial rule](/wiki/Italian_Libya "Italian Libya"), Italian greatly declined under the [rule of Muammar Gaddafi](/wiki/History_of_Libya_under_Muammar_Gaddafi "History of Libya under Muammar Gaddafi"), who expelled the [Italian Libyan](/wiki/Italian_Libyan "Italian Libyan") population and made [Arabic](/wiki/Modern_Standard_Arabic "Modern Standard Arabic") the sole official language of the country.[[64]](#cite_note-65) A few hundred Italian settlers returned to Libya in the 2000s.

Italian was the official language of [Eritrea](/wiki/Eritrea "Eritrea") during [Italian colonisation](/wiki/Italian_Eritrea "Italian Eritrea"). Italian is today used in commerce, and it is still spoken especially among elders; besides that, Italian words are incorporated as loan words in the main language spoken in the country (Tigrinya). The capital city of Eritrea, [Asmara](/wiki/Asmara "Asmara"), still has several Italian schools, established during the colonial period. In the early 19th century, Eritrea was the country with the highest number of Italians abroad, and the [Italian Eritreans](/wiki/Italian_Eritreans "Italian Eritreans") grew from 4,000 during World War I to nearly 100,000 at the beginning of World War II.[[65]](#cite_note-66) In Asmara there are two Italian schools, the [Istituto Italiano Statale Omnicomprensivo di Asmara](/wiki/Istituto_Italiano_Statale_Omnicomprensivo_di_Asmara "Istituto Italiano Statale Omnicomprensivo di Asmara") (Italian primary school with a [Montessori](/wiki/Montessori "Montessori") department) and the [Liceo Sperimentale "G. Marconi"](/wiki/Istituto_Italiano_Statale_Omnicomprensivo_di_Asmara#Divisions "Istituto Italiano Statale Omnicomprensivo di Asmara") (Italian international senior high school).

Italian was also introduced to [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia") through colonialism and was the sole official language of administration and education during the [colonial period](/wiki/Italian_Somaliland "Italian Somaliland") but fell out of use after government, educational and economic infrastructure were destroyed in the [Somali Civil War](/wiki/Somali_Civil_War "Somali Civil War").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Italian_USC2000_PHS.svg/500px-Italian_USC2000_PHS.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Italian_USC2000_PHS.svg)

[Italian language in the United States](/wiki/Italian_language_in_the_United_States "Italian language in the United States")

Italian is also spoken by large [immigrant and expatriate communities](/wiki/Italian_diaspora "Italian diaspora") in the Americas and Australia.[[1]](#cite_note-e25-1) Although over 17 million [Americans are of Italian descent](/wiki/Italian_American "Italian American"), only a little over one million people in the United States speak Italian at home.[[66]](#cite_note-67) Nevertheless, an Italian language media market does exist in the country.[[67]](#cite_note-68) In Canada, Italian is the second most spoken non-official language when [varieties of Chinese](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese") are not grouped together, with 375,645 claiming Italian as their [mother tongue](/wiki/Demolinguistic_descriptors_used_in_Canada#Mother_tongue "Demolinguistic descriptors used in Canada") in 2016.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

Italian immigrants to South America have also brought a presence of the language to that continent. According to some sources, Italian is the second most spoken language in [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina")[[69]](#cite_note-70) after the official language of Spanish, although its number of speakers, mainly of the older generation, is decreasing. Italian bilingual speakers can be found scattered across the southeast of Brazil and in the south.[[1]](#cite_note-e25-1) In [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela"), Italian is the most spoken language after Spanish and Portuguese, with around 200,000 speakers.[[70]](#cite_note-L'ITALIANO_IN_VENEZUELA-71) In [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay"), people who speak Italian as their home language are 1.1% of the total population of the country.[[71]](#cite_note-72) In Australia, Italian is the second most spoken foreign language after Chinese, with 1.4% of the population speaking it as their home language.[[72]](#cite_note-73)

The main Italian-language newspapers published outside Italy are the *[L'Osservatore Romano](/wiki/L%27Osservatore_Romano "L'Osservatore Romano")* ([Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City")), the *L'Informazione di San Marino* ([San Marino](/wiki/San_Marino "San Marino")), the *[Corriere del Ticino](/wiki/Corriere_del_Ticino "Corriere del Ticino")* and the *[laRegione Ticino](/wiki/LaRegione_Ticino "LaRegione Ticino")* ([Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland")), the *[La Voce del Popolo](/wiki/La_Voce_del_Popolo "La Voce del Popolo")* ([Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia")), the *Corriere d'Italia* (Germany), the *L'italoeuropeo* (United Kingdom), the *Passaparola* ([Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg")), the *[America Oggi](/wiki/America_Oggi "America Oggi")* (United States), the *[Corriere Canadese](/wiki/Corriere_Canadese "Corriere Canadese")* and the *[Corriere Italiano](/wiki/Corriere_Italiano "Corriere Italiano")* (Canada), the *Il punto d'incontro* (Mexico), the *L'Italia del Popolo* ([Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina")), the *Fanfulla* (Brazil), the *Gente d'Italia* ([Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay")), the *La Voce d'Italia* ([Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela")), the *[Il Globo](/wiki/Il_Globo "Il Globo")* (Australia) and the *La gazzetta del Sud Africa* (South Africa).[[73]](#cite_note-74)[[74]](#cite_note-75)[[75]](#cite_note-76)

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Italian_language&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/ItalSchoolRijeka.jpg/250px-ItalSchoolRijeka.jpg)](/wiki/File:ItalSchoolRijeka.jpg)

[Italian Secondary School in Rijeka/Fiume](/wiki/Italian_Secondary_School,_Rijeka "Italian Secondary School, Rijeka"), [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia")

Italian is widely taught in many schools around the world. In the 21st century, technology also allows for the continual spread of the Italian language, as people have new ways to learn how to speak, read, and write languages at their own pace and at any given time. For example, the free website and application [Duolingo](/wiki/Duolingo "Duolingo") has 4.94 million English speakers learning the Italian language.[[76]](#cite_note-77)

According to the [Italian Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Italian_Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs "Italian Ministry of Foreign Affairs"), every year there are more than 200,000 foreign students who study the Italian language; they are distributed among the 90 [Institutes of Italian Culture](/wiki/Italian_Cultural_Institute "Italian Cultural Institute") that are located around the world, in the 179 Italian schools located abroad, or in the 111 Italian lecturer sections belonging to foreign schools where Italian is taught as a language of culture.[[77]](#cite_note-esteri.it-78)

As of 2022, Australia had the highest number of students learning Italian in the world. This occurred because of support by the Italian community in Australia and the Italian Government and also because of successful educational reform efforts led by local governments in Australia.[[78]](#cite_note-79)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Placard_piemont%C3%A8is_a_San_Fransesch_an_Argentin-a.jpg/250px-Placard_piemont%C3%A8is_a_San_Fransesch_an_Argentin-a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Placard_piemont%C3%A8is_a_San_Fransesch_an_Argentin-a.jpg)

Trilingual sign in [San Francisco, Argentina](/wiki/San_Francisco,_Argentina "San Francisco, Argentina"), in Spanish, Italian, and [Piedmontese](/wiki/Piedmontese "Piedmontese")

From the late 19th to the mid-20th century, millions of Italians settled in Argentina, Uruguay, southern Brazil, and Venezuela, and in Canada and the United States, where they formed a physical and cultural presence.

In some cases, colonies were established where variants of regional [languages of Italy](/wiki/Languages_of_Italy "Languages of Italy") were used, and some continue to use this regional language. Examples are [Rio Grande do Sul](/wiki/Rio_Grande_do_Sul "Rio Grande do Sul"), Brazil, where [Talian](/wiki/Talian_dialect "Talian dialect") is used, and the town of [Chipilo](/wiki/Chipilo "Chipilo") near Puebla, Mexico; each continues to use a derived form of [Venetian](/wiki/Venetian_language "Venetian language") dating back to the 19th century. Other examples are [Cocoliche](/wiki/Cocoliche "Cocoliche"), an Italian–Spanish [pidgin](/wiki/Pidgin "Pidgin") once spoken in [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") and especially in [Buenos Aires](/wiki/Buenos_Aires "Buenos Aires"), and [Lunfardo](/wiki/Lunfardo "Lunfardo"). The [Rioplatense Spanish](/wiki/Rioplatense_Spanish "Rioplatense Spanish") dialect of Argentina and Uruguay today has thus been heavily influenced by both standard Italian and Italian regional languages as a result.

### Lingua franca

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Italian_language&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Lingua franca")]

See also: [Mediterranean Lingua Franca](/wiki/Mediterranean_Lingua_Franca "Mediterranean Lingua Franca")

Starting in late [medieval](/wiki/Medieval "Medieval") times in much of Europe and the Mediterranean, Latin was replaced as the primary commercial language by languages of Italy, especially Tuscan and Venetian. These varieties were consolidated during the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") with the strength of Italy and the rise of [humanism](/wiki/Renaissance_humanism "Renaissance humanism") and [the arts](/wiki/The_arts "The arts").

Italy came to enjoy increasing artistic prestige within Europe. A mark of the educated gentlemen was to make the [Grand Tour](/wiki/Grand_Tour "Grand Tour"), visiting Italy to see its great historical monuments and works of art. It was expected that the visitor would learn at least some Italian, understood as language based on Florentine. In England, while the classical languages [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") and [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") were the first to be learned, Italian became the second most common modern language after French, a position it held until the late 18th century when it tended to be replaced by German. [John Milton](/wiki/John_Milton "John Milton"), for instance, wrote some of his early poetry in Italian.

Within the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), Italian is known by a large part of the ecclesiastical hierarchy and is used in substitution for Latin in some official documents.

Italian [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") continue to be used in most languages in matters of art and music (especially classical music including opera), in the design and fashion industries, in some sports such as [football](/wiki/Football_(association) "Football (association)")[[79]](#cite_note-80) and especially in culinary terms.

## Languages and dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Italian_language&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Languages and dialects")]

See also: [Languages of Italy](/wiki/Languages_of_Italy "Languages of Italy") and [Regional Italian](/wiki/Regional_Italian "Regional Italian")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Dialetti_Italia_1939.png/250px-Dialetti_Italia_1939.png)](/wiki/File:Dialetti_Italia_1939.png)

Linguistic map of Italy according to Clemente Merlo and Carlo Tagliavini (1937)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Minoranze_linguistiche_it.svg/250px-Minoranze_linguistiche_it.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Minoranze_linguistiche_it.svg)

Italy's ethno-linguistic minorities[[80]](#cite_note-81)

In Italy, almost all the [other languages spoken as the vernacular](/wiki/Languages_of_Italy "Languages of Italy")—other than standard Italian and some languages spoken among immigrant communities—are often called "[Italian dialects](/wiki/Languages_of_Italy "Languages of Italy")", a label that can be very misleading if it is understood to mean "dialects of Italian". The Romance dialects of Italy are local evolutions of spoken Latin that pre-date the establishment of Italian, and as such are [sister languages](/wiki/Sister_language "Sister language") to the Tuscan that was the historical source of Italian. They can be quite different from Italian and from each other, with some belonging to different linguistic branches of Romance. The only exceptions to this are twelve groups considered "[historical language minorities](/wiki/Minority_languages_of_Italy "Minority languages of Italy")", which are officially recognised as distinct [minority languages](/wiki/Minority_language "Minority language") by the law. On the other hand, [Corsican](/wiki/Corsican_language "Corsican language") (a language spoken on the French island of [Corsica](/wiki/Corsica "Corsica")) is closely related to medieval [Tuscan](/wiki/Tuscan_dialect "Tuscan dialect"), from which standard Italian derives and evolved.

The differences in the evolution of Latin in the different regions of Italy can be attributed to the natural [changes](/wiki/Language_change#Causes "Language change") that all languages in regular use are subject to, and to some extent to the presence of three other types of languages: [substrata, superstrata, and adstrata](/wiki/Stratum_(linguistics) "Stratum (linguistics)"). The most prevalent were substrata (the language of the original inhabitants), as the Italian dialects were most probably simply Latin as spoken by native cultural groups. Superstrata and adstrata were both less important. Foreign conquerors of Italy that dominated different regions at different times left behind little to no influence on the dialects. Foreign cultures with which Italy engaged in peaceful relations with, such as trade, had no significant influence either.[[20]](#cite_note-:0-21): 19–20

Throughout Italy, regional varieties of standard Italian, called [Regional Italian](/wiki/Regional_Italian "Regional Italian"), are spoken. Regional differences can be recognised by various factors: the openness of vowels, the length of the consonants, and influence of the local language (for example, in informal situations **[andà](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/and%C3%A0#Italian "wikt:andà")**, **[annà](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/ann%C3%A0#Italian "wikt:annà")** and **[nare](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/nare#Italian "wikt:nare")** replace the standard Italian **[andare](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/andare#Italian "wikt:andare")** in the area of Tuscany, Rome and Venice respectively for the infinitive 'to go').

There is no definitive date when the various Italian variants of Latin—including varieties that contributed to modern standard Italian—began to be distinct enough from Latin to be considered separate languages. One criterion for determining that two language variants are to be considered separate languages rather than variants of a single language is that they have evolved so that they are no longer [mutually intelligible](/wiki/Mutually_intelligible "Mutually intelligible"); this diagnostic is effective if mutual intelligibility is minimal or absent (e.g. in Romance, Romanian and Portuguese), but it fails in cases such as Spanish-Portuguese or Spanish-Italian, as educated native speakers of either pairing (particularly Spanish-Portuguese) can understand each other well if they choose to do so; however, the level of intelligibility is markedly lower between Italian-Spanish, and considerably higher between the Iberian sister languages of Portuguese-Spanish. Speakers of this latter pair can communicate with one another with remarkable ease, each speaking to the other in his own native language, without slang/jargon.

Nevertheless, on the basis of accumulated differences in morphology, syntax, phonology, and to some extent lexicon, it is not difficult to identify that for the Romance varieties of Italy, the first extant written evidence of languages that can no longer be considered Latin comes from the 9th and 10th centuries CE. These written sources demonstrate certain vernacular characteristics and sometimes explicitly mention the use of the vernacular in Italy.

Full literary manifestations of the vernacular began to surface around the 13th century in the form of various religious texts and poetry.[[20]](#cite_note-:0-21): 21Although these are the first written records of Italian varieties separate from Latin, the spoken language had probably diverged long before the first written records appeared since those who were literate generally wrote in Latin even if they spoke other Romance varieties in person.

Throughout the 19th and 20th centuries, the use of standard Italian became increasingly widespread and was mirrored by a decline in the use of the dialects. An increase in literacy was one of the main driving factors (one can assume that only literates were capable of learning standard Italian, whereas those who were illiterate had access only to their native dialect). The percentage of literates rose from 25% in 1861 to 60% in 1911, and then on to 78.1% in 1951. [Tullio De Mauro](/wiki/Tullio_De_Mauro "Tullio De Mauro"), an Italian linguist, has asserted that in 1861, only 2.5% of the population of Italy could speak standard Italian. He reports that in 1951, that percentage had risen to 87%. The ability to speak Italian did not necessarily mean that it was in everyday use, and most people (63.5%) still usually spoke their native dialects. In addition, other factors such as mass emigration, industrialization, and urbanization, and internal migrations after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), contributed to the proliferation of standard Italian. The Italians who emigrated during the [Italian diaspora](/wiki/Italian_diaspora "Italian diaspora") beginning in 1861 were often of the uneducated lower class, and thus the emigration had the effect of increasing the percentage of literates, who often knew and understood the importance of standard Italian back home in Italy. A large percentage of those who had emigrated also eventually returned to Italy, often more educated than when they had left.[[20]](#cite_note-:0-21): 35

Although use of the Italian dialects has declined in the [modern era](/wiki/Modern_era "Modern era"), as Italy unified under standard Italian and continues to do so aided by mass media from newspapers to radio to television, [diglossia](/wiki/Diglossia "Diglossia") is still frequently encountered in Italy and [triglossia](/wiki/Triglossia "Triglossia") is not uncommon in emigrant communities among older speakers. Both situations normally involve some degree of [code-switching](/wiki/Code-switching "Code-switching") and [code-mixing](/wiki/Code-mixing "Code-mixing").[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrifti2014-82)

| [Approximant](/wiki/Approximant_consonant "Approximant consonant") |  | |  | |  | [j](/wiki/Voiced_palatal_approximant "Voiced palatal approximant") |  | [w](/wiki/Voiced_labial%E2%80%93velar_approximant "Voiced labial–velar approximant") |
| [Lateral](/wiki/Lateral_consonant "Lateral consonant") |  | |  | [l](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_lateral_approximant "Voiced alveolar lateral approximant") |  | [ʎ](/wiki/Voiced_palatal_lateral_approximant "Voiced palatal lateral approximant") |  | |
| [Trill](/wiki/Trill_consonant "Trill consonant") |  | |  | [r](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_trill "Voiced alveolar trill") |  | |  | |

Notes:

* Between two vowels, or between a vowel and an approximant (/j, w/) or a liquid (/l, r/), consonants can be both singleton or [geminate](/wiki/Gemination "Gemination"). Geminate consonants shorten the preceding vowel (or block phonetic lengthening) and the first element of the geminate is [unreleased](/wiki/Stop_consonant "Stop consonant"). For example, compare /fato/ [ˈfaːto] ('fate') with /fatto/ [ˈfat̚to] ('fact' or 'did'/'done').[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHall194477–78-83)[[83]](#cite_note-84) However, /ɲ/, /ʃ/, /ʎ/, /d͡z/, /t͡s/ are always geminate intervocalically, including across word boundaries.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERogersd'Arcangeli2004117-85) Similarly, nasals, liquids, and sibilants are pronounced slightly longer in medial consonant clusters.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHall194478-86)
* /j/, /w/, and /z/ are the only consonants that cannot be geminated.
* /t, d/ are [laminal](/wiki/Laminal_consonant "Laminal consonant") [denti-alveolar](/wiki/Denti-alveolar_consonant "Denti-alveolar consonant") [[t̪](/wiki/Voiceless_dental_plosive "Voiceless dental plosive"), [d̪](/wiki/Voiced_dental_plosive "Voiced dental plosive")],[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBertinettoLoporcaro2005132-87)[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECanepari199262-88)[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERogersd'Arcangeli2004117-85) commonly called "dental" for simplicity.
* /k, ɡ/ are pre-velar before /i, e, ɛ, j/.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECanepari199262-88)
* /t͡s, d͡z, s, z/ have two variants:
  + Dentalized laminal [alveolar](/wiki/Alveolar_consonant "Alveolar consonant") [[t̪͡s̪](/wiki/Voiceless_dental_sibilant_affricate "Voiceless dental sibilant affricate"), [d̪͡z̪](/wiki/Voiced_dental_sibilant_affricate "Voiced dental sibilant affricate"), [s̪](/wiki/Voiceless_dental_sibilant_fricative "Voiceless dental sibilant fricative"), [z̪](/wiki/Voiced_dental_sibilant_fricative "Voiced dental sibilant fricative")][[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBertinettoLoporcaro2005132-87)[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECanepari199268,_75–76-89) (commonly called "dental" for simplicity), pronounced with the blade of the tongue very close to the upper front teeth, with the tip of the tongue resting behind lower front teeth.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECanepari199268,_75–76-89)
  + Non-retracted [apical](/wiki/Apical_consonant "Apical consonant") alveolar [[t͡s̺](/wiki/Voiceless_apico-alveolar_affricate "Voiceless apico-alveolar affricate"), [d͡z̺](/wiki/Voiced_apico-alveolar_affricate "Voiced apico-alveolar affricate"), [s̺](/wiki/Voiceless_apico-alveolar_fricative "Voiceless apico-alveolar fricative"), [z̺](/wiki/Voiced_apico-alveolar_fricative "Voiced apico-alveolar fricative")].[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECanepari199268,_75–76-89) The stop component of the "apical" affricates is actually laminal denti-alveolar.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECanepari199268,_75–76-89)
* /n, l, r/ are apical alveolar [[n̺](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_nasal "Voiced alveolar nasal"), [l̺](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_lateral_approximant "Voiced alveolar lateral approximant"), [r̺](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_trill "Voiced alveolar trill")] in most environments.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBertinettoLoporcaro2005132-87)[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERogersd'Arcangeli2004117-85)[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECanepari199257,_84,_88–89-90) /n, l/ are laminal denti-alveolar [[n̪](/wiki/Voiced_dental_nasal "Voiced dental nasal"), [l̪](/wiki/Voiced_dental_lateral_approximant "Voiced dental lateral approximant")] before /t, d, t͡s, d͡z, s, z/[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERogersd'Arcangeli2004117-85)[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBertinettoLoporcaro2005133-91)[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECanepari199258,_88–89-92) and [palatalized](/wiki/Palatalization_(phonetics) "Palatalization (phonetics)") laminal [postalveolar](/wiki/Postalveolar_consonant "Postalveolar consonant") [n̠ʲ, l̠ʲ] before /t͡ʃ, d͡ʒ, ʃ/.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBertinettoLoporcaro2005134-93)[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECanepari199257–59,_88–89-94)[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Italian_language#Dubious "Talk:Italian language")*] /n/ is velar [[ŋ](/wiki/Voiced_velar_nasal "Voiced velar nasal")] before /k, ɡ/.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBertinettoLoporcaro2005134–135-95)[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECanepari199259-96)
* /m/ and /n/ do not contrast before /p, b/ and /f, v/, where they are pronounced [[m](/wiki/Voiced_bilabial_nasal "Voiced bilabial nasal")] and [[ɱ](/wiki/Voiced_labiodental_nasal "Voiced labiodental nasal")], respectively.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBertinettoLoporcaro2005134–135-95)[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECanepari199258-97)
* /ɲ/ and /ʎ/ are [alveolo-palatal](/wiki/Alveolo-palatal_consonant "Alveolo-palatal consonant").[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERecasens201313-98) In a large number of accents, /ʎ/ is a fricative [[ʎ̝](/wiki/Voiced_palatal_lateral_fricative "Voiced palatal lateral fricative")].[[98]](#cite_note-99)
* Intervocalically, single /r/ is realised as a trill with one or two contacts.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELadefogedMaddieson1996221-100) Some literature treats the single-contact trill as a [tap](/wiki/Tap_consonant "Tap consonant") [[ɾ](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_tap_or_flap "Voiced alveolar tap or flap")].[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERogersd'Arcangeli2004118-101)[[101]](#cite_note-Italian_phonology_A_Handbook_of_Pronunciation-102) Single-contact trills can also occur elsewhere, particularly in unstressed syllables.[[102]](#cite_note-103) Geminate /rr/ manifests as a trill with three to seven contacts.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELadefogedMaddieson1996221-100)
* The phonemic distinction between /s/ and /z/ is neutralized before consonants and at the beginning of words: the former is used before voiceless consonants and before vowels at the beginning of words; the latter is used before voiced consonants. The two can contrast only between vowels within a word, e.g. *fuso* /ˈfuzo/ 'melted' versus *fuso* /ˈfuso/ 'spindle'. According to Canepari,[[101]](#cite_note-Italian_phonology_A_Handbook_of_Pronunciation-102) although, the *traditional* standard has been replaced by a modern *neutral pronunciation* which always prefers /z/ when intervocalic, except when the intervocalic *s* is the initial sound of a word, if the compound is still felt as such: for example, *presento* /preˈsɛnto/[[103]](#cite_note-104) ('I foresee', with *pre-* meaning 'before' and *sento* meaning 'I perceive') vs *presento* /preˈzɛnto/[[104]](#cite_note-105) ('I present'). There are many words for which dictionaries now indicate that both pronunciations, either [z] or [s], are acceptable. Word-internally between vowels, the two phonemes have merged in many regional varieties of Italian, as either /z/ (northern-central) or /s/ (southern-central).

Italian has a seven-vowel system, consisting of /a, ɛ, e, i, ɔ, o, u/, and 23 consonants. Compared with most other Romance languages, Italian phonology is conservative, preserving many words nearly unchanged from [Vulgar Latin](/wiki/Vulgar_Latin "Vulgar Latin"). Some examples:

* Italian **[quattordici](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/quattordici#Italian "wikt:quattordici")** 'fourteen' < Latin *[quattuordecim](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/quattuordecim#Latin "wikt:quattuordecim")* (cf. Spanish **[catorce](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/catorce#Spanish "wikt:catorce")**, French **[quatorze](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/quatorze#French "wikt:quatorze")** /katɔʁz/, [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language") and [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") *[catorze](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/catorze "wikt:catorze")*)
* Italian *settimana* 'week' < Latin septimāna (cf. Romanian *săptămână*, Spanish and Portuguese *semana*, French *semaine* /səmɛn/, [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language") *setmana*)
* Italian *medesimo* 'same' < Vulgar Latin \*medi(p)simum (cf. Spanish *mismo*, [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") *mesmo*, French *même* /mɛm/, [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language") *mateix*; Italian usually prefers the shorter *stesso*)
* Italian *guadagnare* 'to win, earn, gain' < Vulgar Latin \*guadaniāre < [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages") /waidanjan/ (cf. Spanish *ganar*, Portuguese *ganhar*, French *gagner* /ɡaɲe/, [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language") *guanyar*).

The conservative nature of Italian phonology is partly explained by its origin. Italian stems from a literary language that is derived from the 13th-century speech of the city of [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence") in the region of [Tuscany](/wiki/Tuscany "Tuscany"), and has changed little in the last 700 years or so. Furthermore, the Tuscan dialect is the most conservative of all [Italian dialects](/wiki/Regional_Italian "Regional Italian"), radically different from the [Gallo-Italian languages](/wiki/Gallo-Italian_languages "Gallo-Italian languages") less than 160 kilometres (100 mi) to the north (across the [La Spezia–Rimini Line](/wiki/La_Spezia%E2%80%93Rimini_Line "La Spezia–Rimini Line")).

The following are some of the conservative phonological features of Italian, as compared with the common [Western Romance](/wiki/Western_Romance "Western Romance") languages (French, Spanish, [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), [Galician](/wiki/Galician_language "Galician language"), [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language")). Some of these features are also present in [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language").

* Little or no [phonemic](/wiki/Phonemic "Phonemic") [lenition](/wiki/Lenition "Lenition") of consonants between vowels, e.g. vīta > *vita* 'life' (cf. Romanian *viață*, Spanish *vida* [ˈbiða], French *vie*), pedem > *piede* 'foot' (cf. Spanish *pie*, French *pied* /pje/).
  + Words that are an exception to this rule exist, such as: scvtella > *scodella* 'bowl', recipere > *ricevere* 'receive', lacvs > *lago* 'lake', acvs > *ago* 'needle', (only in the [Tuscan](/wiki/Tuscan_dialect "Tuscan dialect") accent and historical standard Italian) vīsus > *viso* /vizo/ 'face'.[[105]](#cite_note-106)
* Preservation of geminate consonants, e.g. annum > /ˈanːo/ *anno* 'year' (cf. Spanish *año* /ˈaɲo/, French *an* /ɑ̃/, Romanian *an*, Portuguese *ano* /ˈɐnu/).
* Preservation of all [Proto-Romance](/wiki/Proto-Romance "Proto-Romance") final vowels, e.g. pacem > *pace* 'peace' (cf. Romanian *pace*, Spanish *paz*, French *paix* /pɛ/), octō > *otto* 'eight' (cf. Romanian *opt*, Spanish *ocho*, French *huit* /ɥi(t)/), fēcī > *feci* 'I did' (cf. Romanian dialectal *feci*, Spanish *hice*, French *fis* /fi/).
* Preservation of most intertonic vowels (those between the stressed syllable and either the beginning or ending syllable). This accounts for some of the most noticeable differences, as in the forms *quattordici* and *settimana* given above.
* Slower consonant development, e.g. folia > Italo-Western /fɔʎʎa/ > *foglia* /ˈfɔʎʎa/ 'leaf' (cf. Romanian *foaie* /ˈfo̯aje/, Spanish *hoja* /ˈoxa/, French *feuille* /fœj/; but note Portuguese *folha* /ˈfoʎɐ/).

Compared with most other Romance languages, Italian has many inconsistent outcomes, where the same underlying sound produces different results in different words, e.g. laxāre > *lasciare* and *lassare*, captiāre > *cacciare* and *cazzare*, (ex)dēroteolāre > *sdrucciolare*, *druzzolare* and *ruzzolare*, rēgīna > *regina* and *reina*. Although in all these examples the second form has fallen out of usage, the dimorphism is thought to reflect the several-hundred-year period during which Italian developed as a literary language divorced from any native-speaking population, with an origin in 12th/13th-century Tuscan but with many words borrowed from [languages](/wiki/Languages_of_Italy "Languages of Italy") farther to the north, with different sound outcomes. (The [La Spezia–Rimini Line](/wiki/La_Spezia%E2%80%93Rimini_Line "La Spezia–Rimini Line"), the most important [isogloss](/wiki/Isogloss "Isogloss") in the entire Romance-language area, passes only about 30 kilometres or 20 miles north of Florence.) Dual outcomes of Latin /p t k/ between vowels, such as lŏcvm > *luogo* but fŏcvm > *fuoco*, was once thought to be due to borrowing of northern voiced forms, but is now generally viewed as the result of early phonetic variation within Tuscany.

Some other features that distinguish Italian from the Western Romance languages:

* Latin ce-,ci- becomes /tʃe, tʃi/ rather than /(t)se, (t)si/.
* Latin -ct- becomes /tt/ rather than /jt/ or /tʃ/: octō > *otto* 'eight' (cf. Spanish *ocho*, French *huit,* Portuguese *oito*).
* Vulgar Latin -cl- becomes *cchi* /kkj/ rather than /ʎ/: oclum > *occhio* 'eye' (cf. Portuguese *olho* /ˈoʎu/, French *œil* /œj/ < /œʎ/); but Romanian *ochi* /okʲ/.
* Final /s/ is not preserved, and vowel changes rather than /s/ are used to mark the plural: *amico*, *amici* 'male friend(s)', *amica*, *amiche* 'female friend(s)' (cf. Romanian *amic*, *amici* and *amică*, *amice*; Spanish *amigo(s)* 'male friend(s)', *amiga(s)* 'female friend(s)'); trēs, sex → *tre, sei* 'three, six' (cf. Romanian *trei*, *șase*; Spanish *tres*, *seis*).

Standard Italian also differs in some respects from most nearby Italian languages:

* Perhaps most noticeable is the total lack of [metaphony](/wiki/Metaphony_(Romance_languages) "Metaphony (Romance languages)"), although metaphony is a feature characterizing nearly every other [Italian language](/wiki/Languages_of_Italy "Languages of Italy").
* No simplification of original /nd/, /mb/ (which often became /nn/, /mm/ elsewhere).
